content:
# Exodus Chapter 10 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~36v–37r (PDF pp. 71–72); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 50–51 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_010_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_010_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_010_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Eighth Plague: Locusts (vv. 1–20)

**1** And the LORD said to Moses, "Go in to Pharaoh, for I have made heavy [**kaved**, Hiphil] his heart and the heart of his servants, so that I may set these signs of Mine in his midst,

**2** and so that you may recount in the ears of your son and of your son's son what I have dealt upon Egypt, and My signs that I have set among them — and you shall know that I am the LORD."

**3** And Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh and said to him, "Thus says the LORD, the God of the Hebrews: 'How long will you refuse to humble yourself before Me? Send out My people, that they may serve Me.

**4** For if you refuse to send out My people, behold, I am bringing tomorrow the locust [**arbeh**] into your territory.

**5** And it shall cover the eye of the land, and no one shall be able to see the land. And it shall eat the remnant of what has escaped — that which remains to you from the hail — and it shall eat every tree that sprouts for you from the field.

**6** And they shall fill your houses, and the houses of all your servants, and the houses of all Egypt — that which your fathers and your fathers' fathers have not seen, from the day they were upon the ground until this day.'" And he turned and went out from Pharaoh.

**7** And the servants of Pharaoh said to him, "How long shall this one be a snare to us? Send out the men, that they may serve the LORD their God. Do you not yet know that Egypt is destroyed?"

**8** And Moses and Aaron were brought back to Pharaoh, and he said to them, "Go, serve the LORD your God. Who exactly are the ones going?"

**9** And Moses said, "With our young and with our old we will go; with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds we will go, for it is a feast of the LORD for us."

**10** And he said to them, "May the LORD be so with you — as I would send you out with your little ones! See, for evil is before your faces.

**11** Not so! Go now, the men, and serve the LORD, for that is what you are seeking." And he drove them out from the presence of Pharaoh.

**12** And the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt for the locust, and it shall come up over the land of Egypt and eat every plant of the land — all that the hail left remaining."

**13** And Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt, and the LORD drove an east wind upon the land all that day and all that night. Morning came, and the east wind carried the locust.

**14** And the locust came up over all the land of Egypt and rested upon all the territory of Egypt — very heavy [**kaved me'od**]. Before it there had not been such a locust as this, and after it there shall not be so.

**15** And it covered the eye of all the land, and the land was darkened. And it ate every plant of the land and every fruit of the tree that the hail had left remaining. And nothing green was left remaining in the tree or in the plant of the field in all the land of Egypt.

**16** And Pharaoh hastened to call for Moses and for Aaron and said, "I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you.

**17** And now, bear, I pray, my sin only this time, and entreat the LORD your God, and let Him remove from upon me only this death."

**18** And he went out from Pharaoh and entreated the LORD.

**19** And the LORD turned a west wind, very strong, and it carried the locust and thrust it toward the Sea of Reeds. Not one locust remained in all the territory of Egypt.

**20** **And the LORD strengthened [chazaq, Piel] the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not send out the sons of Israel.**

## The Ninth Plague: Darkness (vv. 21–29)

**21** And the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand toward the heavens, and let there be darkness [**choshekh**] over the land of Egypt — a darkness to be felt [**yammesh choshekh**]."

**22** And Moses stretched out his hand toward the heavens, and there was a thick darkness [**choshekh-afelah**] in all the land of Egypt three days.

**23** They did not see one another, and no one rose from his place three days. But for all the sons of Israel there was light in their dwellings.

**24** And Pharaoh called to Moses and said, "Go, serve the LORD. Only your flocks and your herds shall be held back. Also your little ones shall go with you."

**25** And Moses said, "You yourself must also give into our hands sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may offer them to the LORD our God.

**26** And also our livestock shall go with us; not a hoof shall remain behind, for from them we shall take to serve the LORD our God, and we — we do not know with what we shall serve the LORD until we come there."

**27** **And the LORD strengthened [chazaq, Piel] the heart of Pharaoh, and he was not willing to send them out.**

**28** And Pharaoh said to him, "Go from me! Guard yourself — do not again see my face, for on the day you see my face you shall die."

**29** And Moses said, "You have spoken rightly; I shall not again see your face."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~36v (PDF p. 71) opens with the beginning of Exodus 10, continuing from the close of chapter 9 in the standard three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. A setumah (closed paragraph) marker appears at the transition from the hail plague conclusion (9:35) into the divine speech that opens the locust plague (10:1). The text of the locust narrative spans the remainder of this folio. The prominent parashah marker between the locust section and the darkness section (v. 21) is a petuchah (open paragraph), indicating a major thematic break — the transition from biological plague (locusts) to cosmic plague (darkness). The second page (folio ~37r, PDF p. 72) continues with the darkness narrative and the final confrontation between Moses and Pharaoh (vv. 24–29), then transitions into the beginning of chapter 11. Masorah parva notes appear in the margins, including notations on the rare form veyammesh (v. 21) and on the locust description kaved me'od (v. 14). The Codex Vaticanus pages 50–51 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 10. Page 50 (continuing from chapter 9's conclusion in the previous columns) carries the locust plague narrative, and page 51 continues with the darkness plague and the confrontation sequence through to the end of the chapter. The chapter marker for chapter 10 is visible in the Vaticanus margin. The LXX text shows several notable divergences from the MT, particularly in the intergenerational testimony statement of v. 2 and in the description of the darkness in vv. 21–22, as detailed below.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"I have made heavy his heart" (v. 1) — kaved, Hiphil with YHWH as agent — SECOND DIVINE HARDENING:** Hebrew ki-ani hikhbadti et-libbo ve'et-lev avadav. This is the second instance of direct divine hardening in the plague narrative, and the first time the root kaved (rather than chazaq) appears with YHWH as the explicit causal agent. The Hiphil of kaved means "to make heavy, to cause to be weighty." Up to this point, the Hiphil of kaved has been used only with Pharaoh as agent (8:15, 8:32, 9:34 — "he made heavy his heart"). Now God claims this same verb for Himself: "I have made heavy his heart." The addition of "and the heart of his servants" (ve'et-lev avadav) extends the divine hardening beyond Pharaoh to his entire court — the same court that in v. 7 will paradoxically recognize Egypt's destruction. The purpose clause that follows (lema'an shiti otothai elleh beqirbo, "so that I may set these signs of Mine in his midst") reveals the divine purpose: the hardening serves a sign-function. God hardens in order to create the occasion for signs that will generate knowledge of YHWH. LOGOS renders "I have made heavy" to maintain the kaved = "heavy/weighty" convention while marking YHWH as the grammatical subject. The LXX renders egō gar esklēruna autou tēn kardian ("for I have hardened his heart"), using sklērunō ("to harden"), consistent with its rendering of chazaq Piel in 9:12.

2. **"That you may recount in the ears of your son" (v. 2) — intergenerational testimony:** Hebrew ulema'an tesapper be'ozney binkha uven-binkha et asher hit'alalti beMitsrayim ve'et-otothai asher-samti vam vida'tem ki-ani YHWH. The verb tesapper (Piel of saphar, "to count, to recount, to tell") introduces the purpose of intergenerational testimony. The plagues are not merely punitive — they generate a narrative to be transmitted across generations. The phrase be'ozney ("in the ears of") is an idiom of direct, personal telling: the story must be spoken into the ears of children and grandchildren. The verb hit'alalti (Hitpael of 'alal, "to deal with, to act upon") has a strong connotation: "what I have dealt/wrought upon Egypt" — it can carry the sense of "made a mockery of" or "dealt severely with." This single verse establishes the Passover Haggadah's theological foundation: the plagues exist to be told. LOGOS renders "recount in the ears of" to preserve the Hebrew idiom. The LXX renders hopōs diēgēsēsthe eis ta ōta tōn teknōn humōn ("so that you may narrate into the ears of your children"), closely following the MT.

3. **"How long will you refuse to humble yourself before Me?" (v. 3) — le'anot:** Hebrew ad-mathai me'anta le'anot mippanay. The infinitive le'anot (from 'anah, "to be humble, to afflict, to bow low") is the same root used for afflicting oneself on the Day of Atonement (Lev. 16:29, 31; 23:27). God demands that Pharaoh humble/afflict himself before the divine presence — the exact opposite of the mistolel ("self-exaltation") charged in 9:17. The question ad-mathai ("how long?") implies this has been asked repeatedly and patience is nearing its end. LOGOS renders "humble yourself" to capture the reflexive sense of 'anah. The LXX renders entrepesthai me ("to be ashamed/reverent before Me"), using entrepō, which carries the sense of showing deference or respect.

4. **"The locust" (v. 4) — arbeh:** Hebrew hinni mevi machar arbeh bigvulekha. The noun arbeh derives from the root r-b-h ("to be many, to multiply") — the locust is named for its defining characteristic: overwhelming multiplication. The singular collective arbeh ("locust" as a species-mass) emphasizes its nature as an undifferentiated swarm. The locust plague is the climactic biological plague — destroying what the hail left intact (cf. v. 5, "the remnant of what has escaped"), completing the agricultural devastation. LOGOS renders "the locust" (singular collective) rather than "locusts" to preserve the Hebrew number. The LXX renders akrida ("locust"), also singular.

5. **"It shall cover the eye of the land" (v. 5) — et-ein ha'arets:** Hebrew vekhissah et-ein ha'arets velo yukhal lir'ot et-ha'arets. The phrase ein ha'arets ("eye of the land") is a striking metaphor: the land has an "eye" — its visible surface — and the locusts will cover it so completely that one cannot "see the land." The land is effectively blinded, its face hidden beneath the swarm. The metaphor anticipates the darkness plague that follows: first the land's surface is obscured (by locusts), then vision itself is abolished (by darkness). LOGOS renders "the eye of the land" literally, preserving the Hebrew metaphor. The LXX renders to eidos tēs gēs ("the appearance/form of the earth"), interpreting "eye" as "appearance."

6. **"The remnant of what has escaped" (v. 5) — she'erith hapeletah:** Hebrew ve'akhal et-yeter hapeletah hannish'ereth lakhem min-habbarad. The vocabulary here links the plagues as a sequence: yeter ("remainder, excess") + hapeletah ("the escape, the deliverance") + hannish'ereth ("that which remains"). What survived the hail — itself described as unprecedented (9:18, 24) — will now be consumed by the locust. The three near-synonymous terms for "what is left" intensify the sense of progressive, systematic destruction. The late-ripening wheat and spelt that escaped the hail (9:32) are now targeted. LOGOS renders "the remnant of what has escaped" to capture the layered Hebrew vocabulary. The LXX renders to kataleiphthēn ("that which was left remaining"), simplifying the three-term Hebrew construction.

7. **"Egypt is destroyed" (v. 7) — avdah Mitsrayim:** Hebrew haterem teda ki avdah Mitsrayim. This is one of the most dramatic moments in the plague narrative. Pharaoh's own servants break ranks and challenge their king. The verb avdah (Qal perfect of 'avad, "to perish, to be destroyed") is a completed action in Hebrew — not "Egypt is being destroyed" or "Egypt will be destroyed" but "Egypt is destroyed" — it is already done. The question haterem teda ("do you not yet know?") is bitterly ironic: the plagues' stated purpose is "so that you may know" (lema'an teda), and Pharaoh's servants are asking whether their king has achieved even this basic awareness. The word moqesh ("snare, trap") identifies Moses as a source of entrapment — but the question implies that Pharaoh, not Moses, is the true cause of Egypt's ruin. LOGOS renders "Egypt is destroyed" to preserve the completed aspect. The LXX renders apolōlen Aiguptos ("Egypt has perished"), also using the perfect.

8. **"Who exactly are the ones going?" (v. 8) — mi vami haholkhim:** Hebrew mi vami haholkhim. Literally "who and who are the ones going?" — an emphatic interrogative that demands a precise accounting. Pharaoh is negotiating: he suspects (correctly) that Moses intends to take the entire population, and he wants to limit the departure to adult males. The doubled mi vami is colloquial and forceful — "just who exactly is going?" LOGOS renders "who exactly are the ones going?" to capture the emphatic doubling. The LXX renders tines de kai tines hoi poreuomenoi ("who then and who are the ones going?"), preserving the doubling.

9. **"May the LORD be so with you" (v. 10) — yehi ken YHWH immakhem:** Hebrew vayyomer alehem yehi ken YHWH immakhem ka'asher ashalach etkhem ve'et-tappekhem re'u ki ra'ah neged peneykhem. This is a deeply ironic or sarcastic statement. Pharaoh invokes YHWH's name in what appears to be a blessing formula ("May the LORD be so with you") but then adds a conditional that negates it: "as [if] I would send you out with your little ones!" The phrase re'u ki ra'ah neged peneykhem ("See, for evil is before your faces") is ambiguous: ra'ah can mean (a) "evil intent" — Pharaoh accuses Moses of plotting something nefarious; or (b) "calamity" — Pharaoh warns that disaster awaits them. Most likely Pharaoh is accusing Moses of evil intent. LOGOS renders the blessing formula literally and notes the ironic force. The LXX renders idete hoti ponēria proskeistai humin ("see that evil intent is set before you"), reading ra'ah as malicious intent.

10. **"The LORD drove an east wind" (v. 13) — ruach qadim:** Hebrew vaYHWH nihag ruach qadim ba'arets kol-hayyom hahu vekhol-hallaylah. The verb nihag (Piel of nahag, "to drive, to lead") presents YHWH as the driver of the wind — the same verb used for driving livestock or leading a flock. The east wind (ruach qadim) comes from the eastern desert and is characteristically hot, dry, and destructive. That the wind blows "all that day and all that night" creates a sustained, relentless build-up before the locust arrival at morning. LOGOS renders "drove an east wind" to preserve the active divine agency in the wind. The LXX renders kai kurios epēgagen anemon noton ("and the Lord brought up a south wind") — notably reading notos ("south") rather than the MT's qadim ("east"). This LXX divergence reflects Egyptian geography: from Egypt's perspective, locusts characteristically arrive from the south (Sudan/Ethiopia) rather than the east. This is a significant geographical adaptation in the LXX.

11. **"Very heavy" (v. 14) — kaved me'od:** Hebrew kaved me'od lephanav lo hayah khen arbeh kamouhu ve'acharav lo yihyeh ken. The adjective kaved ("heavy, weighty, massive") describes the locust swarm — the same root used throughout the hardening narrative. The verbal echo is intentional: Pharaoh's heart has been "made heavy" (kaved), and now the plague itself is "heavy" (kaved). The kaved-ness of the heart produces kaved-ness of the plague. The formula "before it there had not been... and after it there shall not be" is the language of absolute uniqueness — cf. Joel 1:2; 2:2, which alludes to this very locust plague. LOGOS renders "very heavy" to maintain the kaved connection throughout the chapter. The LXX renders polla sphodra ("very many"), losing the kaved wordplay.

12. **"The land was darkened" (v. 15) — vattech'shakh ha'arets:** Hebrew vattech'shakh ha'arets. The verb chashakh ("to be dark, to grow dark") anticipates the darkness plague that immediately follows (vv. 21–29). The locusts produce a darkness of their own — the land is darkened by the sheer density of the swarm covering its surface. This creates a narrative bridge: locust-darkness leads to cosmic darkness. The ecological note "nothing green was left remaining" (velo-notar kol-yereq) uses yereq ("green, greenness") — the total absence of green in Egypt stands in implied contrast with the land of Goshen. LOGOS renders "the land was darkened" to preserve the verbal link to the darkness plague. The LXX renders eskotisthē hē gē ("the land was darkened").

13. **"Only this death" (v. 17) — et-hammavet hazzeh:** Hebrew veyaser me'alay raq et-hammavet hazzeh. Pharaoh refers to the locust plague as "this death" (hammavet hazzeh). The locust swarm is not merely a nuisance — it is death itself, the death of agriculture, the death of Egypt's food supply. By using mavet ("death") Pharaoh implicitly acknowledges that the locust represents existential threat. This is Pharaoh's second confession of sin (the first being 9:27), and again he asks for removal of the immediate threat — a pattern of momentary repentance followed by renewed resistance. LOGOS renders "this death" literally. The LXX renders ton thanaton touton ("this death").

14. **"The Sea of Reeds" (v. 19) — Yam-Suph:** Hebrew vayyitqa'ehu yammah-Suph. The name Yam-Suph (literally "Sea of Reeds/Rushes") appears here for the first time in Exodus, foreshadowing its critical role in chapters 13–15 where Israel will cross it and Egypt's army will drown. The verb vayyitqa'ehu (from taqa', "to thrust, to drive, to fasten") is forceful: the wind "thrusts" or "drives" the locusts into the sea. The west wind (ruach-yam, literally "sea wind") that removes the locusts is the opposite of the east wind that brought them — God reverses His own instrument. LOGOS renders "Sea of Reeds" to preserve the Hebrew suph ("reed, rush") rather than the traditional but inaccurate "Red Sea." The LXX renders eis tēn eruthran thalassan ("into the Red Sea"), following the traditional interpretation of Suph.

15. **"And the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" (v. 20) — chazaq, Piel — THIRD DIVINE HARDENING:** Hebrew vaychazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh. This is the second occurrence of chazaq Piel with YHWH as subject (after 9:12), but the third divine hardening overall (after the kaved Hiphil in 10:1). The pattern is now established: after each plague is removed at Pharaoh's request, God strengthens Pharaoh's heart. The mercy shown in removing the plague is immediately followed by the hardening that prevents release. LOGOS renders "the LORD strengthened" with bold formatting to mark the divine agency. The LXX renders esklērunen kurios tēn kardian Pharaō ("the Lord hardened the heart of Pharaoh").

16. **"A darkness to be felt" (v. 21) — veyammesh choshekh:** Hebrew neteh yadekha al-hashshamayim vihi choshekh al-erets Mitsrayim veyammesh choshekh. This is one of the most debated phrases in Exodus. The verb yammesh (from mashash, "to feel, to grope") in its relationship to choshekh ("darkness") has been understood in two primary ways: (a) "and one may feel the darkness" — the darkness is so thick it is tangible, palpable; (b) "a darkness beyond darkness" — an intensifying construction. The traditional rendering "a darkness that may be felt" captures the extraordinary image: this is not merely absence of light but a positive, physical substance that can be touched. The Waw-consecutive veyammesh suggests the darkness has a tactile dimension — it crosses the boundary between visual and physical sensation. This darkness reverses creation (cf. Gen. 1:2–4, where God separates light from darkness), returning Egypt to primordial chaos. LOGOS renders "a darkness to be felt" to preserve the tactile sense of mashash. The LXX renders psēlaphēton skotos ("gropeable/palpable darkness"), using psēlaphaō ("to grope, to feel by touch"), which preserves the tactile sense admirably.

17. **"Thick darkness" (v. 22) — choshekh-afelah:** Hebrew vayehi choshekh-afelah bekhol-erets Mitsrayim. The compound choshekh-afelah layers two Hebrew words for darkness: choshekh is the standard term (cf. Gen. 1:2), while afelah (from the root '-p-l, "to be dark, thick, gloomy") denotes a deeper, thicker, more terrifying darkness — dense murk. The afelah occurs in theophanic contexts (Deut. 4:11; 5:22, the darkness at Sinai) and eschatological texts (Joel 2:2; Zeph. 1:15, the Day of the LORD). The compounding of these two terms creates a superlative: darkness-of-darkness, the darkest possible dark. This is not natural nightfall but a supernatural, oppressive, paralyzing gloom. LOGOS renders "thick darkness" for choshekh-afelah, with the Hebrew terms noted. The LXX renders skotos kai gnophos ("darkness and murk/gloom"), using two separate Greek words to capture the compound.

18. **"But for all the sons of Israel there was light in their dwellings" (v. 23) — or bemoshevotham:** Hebrew uvekhol-beney Yisra'el hayah or bemoshevotham. The contrast is absolute: Egypt sits in thick, tangible darkness while Israel has "light in their dwellings" (or bemoshevotham). The word moshav ("dwelling, settlement") rather than bayit ("house") suggests the light extends throughout Israel's entire settlement areas. This divine discrimination between darkness for Egypt and light for Israel reprises the distinction pattern established in the swarm (8:22), livestock (9:4), and hail (9:26) plagues. The light/darkness distinction also echoes the creation narrative — God again separates light from darkness (Gen. 1:4), but now ethnically: Israel is in the realm of light, Egypt in the realm of chaos. LOGOS renders literally. The LXX reads pasi de tois huiois Israēl phōs ēn en pasin hois kateginonto ("but for all the sons of Israel there was light in all [places] where they were").

19. **"Not a hoof shall remain behind" (v. 26) — parsah lo tishsha'er:** Hebrew gam-miqnenu yelekh immanu lo tishsha'er parsah. Literally "a hoof shall not remain behind." The parsah ("hoof") is the smallest conceivable unit of livestock — not even a single hoof-print's worth of animal will be left in Egypt. Moses' demand is maximalist and non-negotiable: everything goes. The phrase "we do not know with what we shall serve the LORD until we come there" (ve'anachnu lo-neda mah-na'avod et-YHWH ad-bo'enu shammah) is either genuine — the specific sacrificial requirements await divine revelation at the destination — or strategic: Moses refuses to specify an amount because any specification would become a ceiling for negotiation. Either way, it leaves Pharaoh no room for partial concession. LOGOS renders "not a hoof shall remain behind" literally. The LXX renders ouch hupoleipsometha hoplēn ("we shall not leave behind a hoof").

20. **"And the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh" (v. 27) — chazaq, Piel — FOURTH DIVINE HARDENING:** Hebrew vaychazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh. The identical formula to v. 20 and 9:12 — chazaq Piel with YHWH as subject. This is the fourth divine hardening overall (9:12, 10:1, 10:20, 10:27) and the third using chazaq Piel. The density of divine hardening in chapter 10 is notable: three instances in a single chapter (vv. 1, 20, 27), compared with one in the entire preceding chapter 9. The acceleration signals the approaching climax. LOGOS renders with bold formatting. The LXX renders esklērunen kurios tēn kardian Pharaō.

21. **"On the day you see my face you shall die" (v. 28) — beyom re'otkha phanay tamut:** Hebrew hishamer lekha al-tosef re'ot panay ki beyom re'otkha phanay tamut. Pharaoh's threat is his last direct assertion of royal authority: he claims the power to decree death upon Moses. The irony is devastating: Pharaoh, whose land sits in impenetrable darkness, who has confessed sin twice, whose servants have declared Egypt destroyed, now threatens the agent of the God who controls light and darkness, life and death. The phrase "see my face" (re'ot panay) is courtroom language — to "see the king's face" means to have audience with him (cf. 2 Sam. 14:24, 28, 32; Esth. 1:14). Pharaoh is banishing Moses from the royal court. LOGOS renders literally. The LXX renders hē an hēmera ophthēs moi thanatō apothanē ("on whatever day you appear to me, you shall surely die"), using the cognate accusative construction for emphasis.

22. **"You have spoken rightly" (v. 29) — ken dibbarta:** Hebrew ken dibbarta lo-osif od re'ot panekha. Moses' response — "you have spoken rightly" — is laden with dramatic irony. Moses affirms Pharaoh's words but with a meaning Pharaoh does not intend: indeed, Moses will not see Pharaoh's face again (voluntarily), but not because of Pharaoh's threat. Rather, the final plague will make Pharaoh send for Moses (cf. 12:31) — the dynamic will reverse completely. The adverb ken ("rightly, correctly, so") is an affirmation that simultaneously accepts the surface statement and subverts its intended force. LOGOS renders "you have spoken rightly" to preserve the Ken's affirmative force. The LXX renders eirēkas ("you have spoken"), without the affirmative adverb.

### Hardening Verb Tracking — Exodus 10

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Form | Subject/Agent | LOGOS Rendering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **10:1** | **kaved** | **Hiphil (causative)** | **YHWH makes heavy Pharaoh's heart and his servants' heart** | **"I have made heavy his heart and the heart of his servants"** |
| **10:20** | **chazaq** | **Piel (causative)** | **YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart** | **"the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"** |
| **10:27** | **chazaq** | **Piel (causative)** | **YHWH strengthens Pharaoh's heart** | **"the LORD strengthened the heart of Pharaoh"** |

*Note: Exodus 10 marks a dramatic escalation in the hardening narrative. All three hardening instances in this chapter are divine hardenings — there is no human self-hardening at all. This stands in sharp contrast with previous chapters where Pharaoh's own agency was dominant (8:15, 8:32, 9:34) or where divine and human hardening alternated (chapter 9). The shift to exclusively divine hardening coincides with the final stages of the plague narrative. In v. 1, the divine hardening uses kaved Hiphil for the first time with YHWH as subject — the very verb that was previously Pharaoh's own action. God now does to Pharaoh what Pharaoh had been doing to himself. Furthermore, v. 1 extends the hardening to "the heart of his servants" — despite the fact that these same servants will, in v. 7, recognize Egypt's destruction. The theological tension is deliberate: divine hardening does not eliminate human awareness of reality but overrides the will to act upon that awareness.*

### Cumulative Hardening Verb Table — Exodus 7–10

| Verse | Root | Form | Agent | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7:3 | qashah | Hiphil | God (prospective) | "I will make hard" — announced, not yet enacted |
| 7:13 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after staff sign |
| 7:14 | kaved | Adjective | — (descriptive) | "is heavy" — God's diagnosis |
| 7:22 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after blood plague |
| 8:15 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after frogs removed |
| 8:19 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — after gnats; magicians confess |
| 8:32 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after swarm removed |
| 9:7 | kaved | Qal (adj.) | Heart (descriptive) | "was heavy" — after livestock plague |
| **9:12** | **chazaq** | **Piel** | **GOD (active)** | **"the LORD strengthened" — FIRST DIVINE HARDENING** |
| 9:34 | kaved | Hiphil | Pharaoh (active) | "made heavy" — after hail; labeled "sin" |
| 9:35 | chazaq | Qal | Heart (stative) | "was strengthened" — chapter closing |
| **10:1** | **kaved** | **Hiphil** | **GOD (active)** | **"I have made heavy" — SECOND DIVINE HARDENING; kaved Hiphil now divine; extended to servants** |
| **10:20** | **chazaq** | **Piel** | **GOD (active)** | **"the LORD strengthened" — THIRD DIVINE HARDENING; after locusts removed** |
| **10:27** | **chazaq** | **Piel** | **GOD (active)** | **"the LORD strengthened" — FOURTH DIVINE HARDENING; triggers final confrontation** |

### Key Term Renderings — Exodus 10

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| arbeh | the locust | vv. 4, 12–14, 19; from r-b-h "to multiply"; singular collective |
| ein ha'arets | the eye of the land | vv. 5, 15; the visible surface of the land |
| hapeletah | what has escaped | v. 5; what survived the hail plague |
| moqesh | snare | v. 7; trap, source of entrapment |
| avdah | is destroyed | v. 7; Qal perfect of 'avad "to perish" |
| ruach qadim | east wind | v. 13; hot desert wind from the east |
| kaved me'od | very heavy | v. 14; describing the locust swarm; same root as hardening verb |
| hammavet | this death | v. 17; Pharaoh's term for the locust plague |
| Yam-Suph | Sea of Reeds | v. 19; lit. "Reed Sea"; first occurrence in Exodus |
| ruach-yam | west wind | v. 19; lit. "sea wind"; the wind from the Mediterranean |
| choshekh | darkness | vv. 21–22; the standard Hebrew term (cf. Gen. 1:2) |
| veyammesh choshekh | a darkness to be felt | v. 21; from mashash "to feel, to grope"; tactile darkness |
| afelah | thick darkness | v. 22; deep, dense murk; theophanic darkness |
| or | light | v. 23; in Israel's dwellings; creation-echo |
| parsah | hoof | v. 26; the smallest unit of livestock; totality marker |

---

### Theological Note: The Architecture of Exodus 10

Exodus 10 presents the final two plagues before the death of the firstborn, and together they form a devastating diptych of annihilation. The locust plague completes the agricultural destruction begun by the hail (ch. 9): what the hail left alive the locust devours, until "nothing green was left remaining" (v. 15). The darkness plague then completes the environmental destruction by abolishing light itself — returning Egypt to the primordial state of tohu vavohu (Gen. 1:2), the darkness that covered the face of the deep before God spoke "Let there be light." Egypt's regression through the plagues mirrors creation in reverse: the water turning to blood (plague 1) corrupts the element separated on Day 2; the swarm and livestock plagues attack the creatures made on Days 5–6; the destruction of vegetation (hail + locusts) reverses Day 3; and now the darkness reverses Day 1 — the most fundamental act of creation. Egypt is being un-created.

The chapter also marks a turning point in the diplomatic dynamic. In v. 7, Pharaoh's own court breaks ranks — "Do you not yet know that Egypt is destroyed?" This is the first and only time Pharaoh's servants directly challenge his judgment. The negotiation scenes in vv. 8–11 and 24–26 show Pharaoh attempting increasingly desperate compromises: first he will allow only the men (v. 11), then he concedes the people but withholds the livestock (v. 24). Moses rejects every compromise with escalating firmness, culminating in the absolute demand "not a hoof shall remain behind" (v. 26). The final exchange in vv. 28–29 — Pharaoh's death threat and Moses' calm acceptance — marks the end of negotiation. There will be no more parleys until the death of the firstborn forces Pharaoh's hand (12:31).

The threefold divine hardening in this single chapter (vv. 1, 20, 27) — with no human self-hardening — signals that the narrative has passed a point of no return. The divine purpose announced in v. 1 (signs for intergenerational testimony) and v. 2 (knowledge that "I am the LORD") now governs the entire remaining sequence. Pharaoh is no longer a free negotiating partner but an instrument of divine revelation, hardened precisely so that the final, climactic act — the death of the firstborn and the Exodus itself — may occur.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 10 (pages 50–51) presents several notable features. Most significantly, in v. 13 the LXX reads "south wind" (anemon noton) where the MT reads "east wind" (ruach qadim). This is a deliberate geographical adaptation: in the Egyptian context, locust swarms typically arrive from the south (from the Sahel region, Sudan, and Ethiopia), not from the east (the Arabian desert). The LXX translators, likely working in Alexandria, adjusted the wind direction to match Egyptian experience — a rare instance of the LXX preferring ecological accuracy over fidelity to the Hebrew. In v. 10, the LXX adds an expansion to Pharaoh's sarcastic response, strengthening the warning about evil intent. In v. 19, where the MT describes the west wind as ruach-yam ("sea wind"), the LXX renders anemon sphodron apo thalassēs ("a violent wind from the sea"), adding the intensifying sphodron. The darkness passage (vv. 21–23) is rendered closely, with the notable psēlaphēton skotos ("grope-able darkness") in v. 21 — a rendering that became influential in later Christian interpretation of the ninth plague as a type of spiritual blindness. The Vaticanus manuscript shows a clear section marker at the beginning of the darkness pericope, consistent with the petuchah in the MT tradition. Page 51 concludes with the transition into chapter 11, with the final confrontation between Moses and Pharaoh rendered closely to the MT.
